title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
SIP vs RD
Investing systematically, rather than in a lump sum, has multiple benefits. It is more affordable as you can set aside small amounts regularly. This can particularly help individuals with a limited income. Secondly, it helps build a habit of disciplined savings, which can help in creating a corpus for your financial goals.
When it comes to systematic savings, two avenues stand out – SIPs in mutual funds and RDs. Let’s understand what they are, their difference, and which one is better.
What is a Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)?
A Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) is a mode of investing in a mutual fund. Under SIPs, you undertake to invest a fixed amount at regular intervals in a mutual fund scheme of your choice. For instance, say you want to invest ₹1000 every month in an equity mutual fund. You can start investing through SIPs, wherein the monthly investment would be ₹1000.
You can start a SIP for any tenure, and you can set a standing instruction on your bank account to debit the SIP amount at specified intervals. A SIP calculator can also help you estimate the returns that you can get on your savings.
Advantages of SIP
A SIP has various advantages that make it popular among investors. Some of these advantages are as follows -
Liquidity
Many mutual funds offer liquidity, allowing you to redeem your investments whenever you want. So, while you invest through SIP, you can redeem your investment if you are in need of funds, if there is no lock-in period.
Flexibility
With SIPs, you can choose any investment amount that you can comfortably set aside regularly. This gives you the flexibility to invest as much as you want as long as you meet the minimum SIP criterion. Moreover, you can also choose the investment frequency, which can be monthly, quarterly, or any other frequency that matches your needs.
Higher Returns
If you choose equity mutual funds, you can enjoy a high return potential on your investments. Even in the case of debt or hybrid funds, you can enjoy market-linked returns on your savings.
Tax Benefits
If you start a SIP in ELSS mutual funds, you can enjoy tax deduction on the amount that you invest. This deduction is allowed under Section 80C, which is available under the old tax regime. The maximum deduction that you can claim under this section is ₹1.5 lakhs.
What is a Recurring Deposit (RD)?
A recurring deposit is a savings scheme which allows you to invest in instalments over a specific tenure. This is a fixed-income scheme which offers a fixed rate of interest on your savings. RDs are offered by banks and other financial institutions.
Advantages of RD
Some of the benefits of RDs are as follows -
Guaranteed Returns
Under a recurring deposit, you get guaranteed returns. Your investment is not exposed to the market and is, thus, not prone to market volatility. You get interest at a fixed rate, which makes RDs a safe investment option.
Adjustable Time Horizon
Financial institutions offer flexible tenure options for RDs. Usually, you can start a recurring deposit for 6 months and go up to 10 years.
Easy Investment
Recurring deposits are easily accessible. You can open an RD account online or offline. Moreover, RDs are affordable too. Some banks might allow you to start an RD with as little as ₹100.
Senior Citizen Advantages
RDs offer added benefits for senior citizens. Individuals aged 60 years and above are usually offered higher interest rates than those offered to normal individuals. So, senior citizens can enjoy 0.50% to 0.75% higher interest rates on their deposits. Moreover, interest income earned up to ₹50,000 can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80TTB if senior citizens choose the old tax regime for filing their returns.
SIP vs RD - Which is Better?
When it comes to ranking RDs and SIPs against each other, the answer depends on your preferences and risk appetite. If you do not want market risks and expect assured returns, you can choose RDs. On the other hand, if you don’t mind investment risks and want to earn market-linked returns, SIPs can be a good choice.
Besides your risk appetite, your financial goals are another important consideration when choosing SIP vs RD. Here are two instances why -
Example 1: Building a College Fund (SIP):
If you are building a college fund for your child, you might have a longer investment tenure if your child is young. Over the long-term horizon, investment risks tend to smooth out. You can ride out short-term market volatility and enjoy attractive returns. Thus, you can choose SIPs in equity funds and create a corpus for your child’s higher education.
Example 2: Saving for a Down Payment (RD):
If you are saving for a down payment on a house or vehicle, you might have a short-term investment horizon of a few years. In such cases, RDs can be suitable as they offer guaranteed returns and help you avoid volatility that can erode your savings in uncertain markets.
Key Takeaways
SIPs are a way to invest in mutual fund schemes wherein you invest a fixed amount at regular intervals.
Recurring deposits are also systematic savings options wherein you invest a fixed amount every month and get fixed interest income.
The benefits of SIPs include liquidity, flexibility, attractive returns and tax benefits with ELSS schemes.
The benefits of RDs include assured returns, adjustable duration, easy investment, and higher interest rates and tax benefits on income for senior citizens.
The choice between RDs and SIPs depends on your risk appetite and financial goals.
Conclusion
Both RDs and SIPs have their respective pros and cons. So, assess what each investment avenue means, its benefits and drawbacks, and then match it with your financial goals and risk appetite. You can also choose both RDs and SIPs to create a diversified portfolio. So, plan your finances effectively and use these investment options to inculcate a disciplined savings habit and build a fund for your financial goals.
FAQs
What factors determine the returns from a SIP?
Some factors that affect SIP returns include the type of fund you have selected, portfolio allocation of the chosen fund, fund managers’ performance, market performance, etc.
Is RD a good investment option?
RDs are a good investment option if you want to save in a disciplined manner and want guaranteed returns on your savings.
Can I invest in both RD and SIP at the same time?
Yes, you can invest in both RD and SIP at the same time, as there is no restriction on combining the two. In fact, you can add both these schemes to your portfolio to diversify it across asset classes.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Section 80C (under old tax regime) of the Income Tax Act shall apply. You are requested to consult your tax consultant and obtain independent advice for eligibility before claiming any benefit under the policy
full_pdf_text:
SIP vs RD
Investing systematically, rather than in a lump sum, has multiple benefits. It is more affordable as you can set aside small amounts regularly. This can particularly help individuals with a limited income. Secondly, it helps build a habit of disciplined savings, which can help in creating a corpus for your financial goals.
When it comes to systematic savings, two avenues stand out – SIPs in mutual funds and RDs. Let’s understand what they are, their difference, and which one is better.
What is a Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)?
A Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) is a mode of investing in a mutual fund. Under SIPs, you undertake to invest a fixed amount at regular intervals in a mutual fund scheme of your choice. For instance, say you want to invest ₹1000 every month in an equity mutual fund. You can start investing through SIPs, wherein the monthly investment would be ₹1000.
You can start a SIP for any tenure, and you can set a standing instruction on your bank account to debit the SIP amount at specified intervals. A SIP calculator can also help you estimate the returns that you can get on your savings.
Advantages of SIP
A SIP has various advantages that make it popular among investors. Some of these advantages are as follows -
1. Flexibility
With SIPs, you can choose any investment amount that you can comfortably set aside regularly. This gives you the flexibility to invest as much as you want as long as you meet the minimum SIP criterion. Moreover, you can also choose the investment frequency, which can be monthly, quarterly, or any other frequency that matches your financial needs. 2. Tax Benefits
If you start a SIP in ELSS mutual funds, you can enjoy tax deduction on the amount that you invest1. This deduction is allowed under Section 80C, which is available under the old tax regime1. The maximum deduction that you can claim under this section is ₹1.5 lakhs1.
What is a Recurring Deposit (RD)?
A recurring deposit is a savings scheme which allows you to invest in instalments over a specific tenure. This is a fixed-income scheme which offers a fixed rate of interest on your savings. RDs are offered by banks and other financial institutions.
Advantages of RD
Some of the benefits of RDs are as follows -
1. Guaranteed Returns
Under a recurring deposit, you get guaranteed returns. Your investment is not exposed to the market and is, thus, not prone to market volatility. You get interest at a fixed rate, which makes RDs a safe investment option. 2. Adjustable Time Horizon
Financial institutions offer flexible tenure options for RDs. Usually, you can start a recurring deposit for 6 months and go up to 10 years2. 3. Easy Investment
Recurring deposits are easily accessible. You can open an RD account online or offline. Moreover, RDs are affordable too. Some banks might allow you to start an RD with as little as ₹1002. 4. Senior Citizen Advantages
RDs offer added benefits for senior citizens. Individuals aged 60 years and above are usually offered higher interest rates than those offered to normal individuals. So, senior citizens can enjoy 0.50% to 0.75% higher interest rates on their deposits2. Moreover, interest income earned up to ₹50,000 can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80TTB if senior citizens choose the old tax regime for filing their returns3.
SIP vs RD - Which is Better?
When it comes to ranking RDs and SIPs against each other, the answer depends on your preferences and risk appetite. If you do not want market risks and expect assured returns, you may consider RDs. On the other hand, if you don’t mind investment risks and want to earn market-linked returns, SIPs can be a suitable choice.
Besides your risk appetite, your financial goals are another important consideration when choosing SIP vs RD. Here are two instances why -
1. Example 1: Building a College Fund (SIP):
If you are building a college fund for your child, you might have a longer investment tenure if your child is young. Over such a horizon, short-term market fluctuations often balance out, and equity-based SIPs are commonly used to build a corpus for long-term goals like higher education. This is one way investors approach long-term planning, though the choice depends on individual circumstances.. 2. Example 2: Saving for a Down Payment (RD):
If you are saving for a down payment on a house or vehicle, you might have a short-term investment horizon of a few years. In such cases, RDs can be suitable as they offer guaranteed returns and help you avoid volatility that can erode your savings in uncertain markets.
Key Takeaways
1. SIPs are a way to invest in mutual fund schemes wherein you invest a fixed amount at
regular intervals. 2. Recurring deposits are also systematic savings options wherein you invest a fixed amount
every month and get fixed interest income. 3. The benefits of SIPs include liquidity, flexibility, attractive returns and tax benefits with ELSS
schemes. 4. The benefits of RDs include assured returns, adjustable duration, easy investment, and
higher interest rates and tax benefits on income for senior citizens. 5. The choice between RDs and SIPs depends on your risk appetite and financial goals.
Conclusion
Both RDs and SIPs have their respective pros and cons. So, assess what each investment avenue means, its benefits and drawbacks, and then match it with your financial goals and risk appetite. Both RDs and SIPs in mutual funds are commonly considered to create a diversified portfolio. So, plan your finances effectively and you may use these investment options to inculcate a disciplined savings habit and build a fund for your financial goals.
FAQs
1. What factors determine the returns from a SIP?
Some factors that affect SIP returns include the type of fund you have selected, portfolio allocation of the chosen fund, fund managers’ performance, market performance, etc.
2. Is RD a suitable investment option?
RDs is a suitable investment option if you want to save in a disciplined manner and want guaranteed returns on your savings. 3. Can I invest in both RD and SIP at the same time?
Yes, both RD and SIP at the same time are commonly considered. In fact, you can add both RD and SIP to your portfolio to diversify it across asset classes.
